speaker: interviewer
text: November 16, 1995. We were at the point of discussing your sister in hospital. But you wanted to go back a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to go back. When we came to Brody, we were placed in, in a room with a lot of people there and all. And after about three months being in the ghetto, there was a, a rumor that the liquidation would start. They will take all the Jews from the ghetto away. So the panic was people, you know, didn't know what to do. And my sister decided we should all run away. And again, like I said, my father wasn't willing to, to move. My father built a, built a hiding place in the attic. He built, built a hiding place in a corner room. And you could go through the attic. You could go down to the room. He closed, he closed up the door and the window to that room that they couldn't-- nobody could tell there was a room there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father said he would hide in that room. He didn't want to run away. Of course, my mother wouldn't leave my father. So my mother encouraged me to go with my sister. So we ran away. And she stayed with your other sister, [NON-ENGLISH] and your brother, Aron.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, father, sister, and brother stayed in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- you had one room in the ghetto? You were all living in this one room--

speaker: interviewee
text: All in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other families.

speaker: interviewer
text: With other families?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other families. With two other families. One I remember exactly, Mrs. Geller. I think there was another family. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ran away. We-- I, I-- so then when I ran away, I went to this woman that my mother helped her to get out of jail. And I came there. And she kept me. She said she would keep one person. She couldn't keep two people. She's afraid. So my sister was older, and I was younger. So she asked me to stay there. She felt I was safe there. She-- there was a place for me to stay. And she went to the village to try to hide in the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she met me after a few weeks. And she said she couldn't take it. She, she-- you know, she was so nervous. She said, she-- we heard that there are some people built bunkers in the woods. And the family stayed together. So my sister decided to go back to Brody, to the ghetto, to encourage my father to leave with the family and build a bunker in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get in and out of the ghetto? At night?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- at night. At night you could easily-- there were guards, but there weren't so many guards. So you could sneak out.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards were German?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards were German. And there were also Polish and the Ukrainian police. But there weren't that many. You could run away easily. So my sister went back to the ghetto. When she came back to the ghetto, my middle sister, Lajcie, was in the hospital. And my mother used to go to the hospital. The, the conditions in the hospital was terrible. She said that they slept on the mattresses. There weren't even sheets on the beds. And so my sister couldn't leave. I mean, there was no way of leaving. My sister was in the hospital. So they took care of my sister. They were going to the hospital. And within a short time, the actual liquidation came. When the liquidation came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, my sister run away. And this time she trapped my mother. She insisted my mother should go with her. My father went to the room to hide, but so many people with children walked down there. You know, you had to go up in the attic, through the attic, you walked down to the room, probably through a step ladder. So he didn't feel safe there. So he came out of that hiding place, the room. And he hid in the attic. And another-- that neighbor that lived in our, with us in the room also was hiding there. She had two children, a, a boy and a girl. So she hid in the same attic there. And they hid for, like, for three days. And after three days, my father came out. And the Germans were running around all over and yelling out, you know, Jews come out, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was taken to a working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away, or-- when was he taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: After we left, during that time. Otherwise, I was there three months. So within the next three months, he was taken to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1942, the beginning of 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- we went to, we went to the ghetto in 1942. So that was in 1943, or at the beginning of 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my brother was in the ghetto. And like I said, my father was hiding in, in that attic. And then after a few days, I think three days-- Mrs. Geller survived, and she was telling me that story. After three days, my father gave himself up. He probably couldn't go on without food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he begged the German, give me a drink of water before you shoot me. Of course, he shot him right there on the place. And Mrs. Geller was hiding there in the attic. And after things quieted down, she ran down and ran away. And she came to the village. She was also hiding. But [INAUDIBLE] she was hiding. She was hiding in the woods, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I stayed in the village, and we used to have a very good friend, a Ukrainian woman. Her name was Keroltchikha. So I used to come to her once in a while to hear. I knew if some family would come, they would come to her. And then I came, and she, she used to keep my mother for a few weeks at a time. And I met my mother there. And my mother was telling me what was going on, the three months that we left. In fact, my mother told me a story that she went down on the street one day. And the Germans were grabbing people, whoever came along. And she was caught. And they were placed in a backyard, like. And the guards, German guards, were all around. And they-- the Germans were looking for things to ridicule people. And for some reason, a chicken, a chicken appeared. You know, somebody must have had a chicken in the ghetto. And the chicken ran away or something. And a chicken appeared. And the German yelled out, whoever will catch that chicken will be alive, and the, the other ones will be shot. And people started struggling to catch that chicken. Anyway, he started shooting, just, you know, randomly whoever he felt like shooting. And my mother told me, next to her a woman was shot. And she seen the blood and everything. And they took the people, then loaded the people on trucks. And they took them out on the, in the woods, in the outskirts of Brody. And they unloaded one truck of people. And my mother was on the second truck. And all of a sudden they stopped, and the Germans told these people not to get off the truck. And they sent the truck back to the ghetto. And she doesn't know really why and what. But apparently those people, the first truck that got off into the woods, they probably shot them there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a miracle. That's how my mother survived at that time. And she was lucky. Now, when the liquidation came and she ran out, down from the room, she went downstairs to the basement. And they buried themselves in the basement, like. They lay down, and there was earth, dirt on the floor. They, with their hands, they pulled the earth from underneath them, and-- they wore black shawls, like, you know, like the peasants had. Excuse me. And they covered themselves with the earth. They threw the earth on their backs. And that's how they covered themselves. And they lay there motionly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Motionless.

speaker: interviewee
text: Motionless. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They lay there overnight. And the Germans were running around in the cellars again, you know, calling, [NON-ENGLISH], Jews come out. And they weren't discovered. And then my sister decided to go to the other cellar where the brewery was. She felt maybe the other cellar is safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she thought she'll go to take a look there, and she'll come back for my mother. And she never came back. And my mother was still laying there for another 24 hours. And when things calmed down, she heard no shooting. The following night my mother tried to get out from the ghetto. She ran away. And she came to Czernicia. So we used to hide in Czernicia separately. We felt if two people come for food, they, they, they, they don't want to give two people food. And, and one person will be easier. So we separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother stayed with Keroltchikha for a few weeks. She would keep her for a week or two. So, so I used to go every night. That's how I used to hide. I would go every night to a different peasant. So the, the people were afraid to keep me in the house. I used to go up in the barn, in the hay, I used to sleep. And at night, I used to go down to the peasants and ask them for a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all knew you were around?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew me. They knew me because it was the same village. And I sort of knew who to go to, who are, who are friends and who are not friends, so-- and some peasants would even tell me, don't go to this one, and that, you know. Some were, some were more anti-Semitic than others. And my mother used to hide the same way, in different places. And she used to stay at this woman's, Keroltchikha's, house quite often. I used to meet her. This was the meeting place. I used to come there and meet her sometimes and, and find out maybe another family member survived. We didn't know what happened to our brother. And one day I was sitting in Keroltchikha's house. In a pigsty, I was hiding. She was very good. She kept, kept me in the pigsty. And she used bring me hot soup there. And that's the only place I got a little warm food. And she came in, very upset. And she said that she seen my brother walk through the village, that a guard that, that works, worked in the, in the woods, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] apparently, he caught him in the woods. My brother ran away from the camp with a friend. And he was hiding in the village woods. And, and this, this guard caught him and took him to the police. So she described that his-- he had his hands raised. And the guard had a, a rifle. And that's how he-- and he was skin and bones. She was, she was a really good soul, this woman. She was really very understanding. And she was almost crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wanted to run out. So she says, where are you going to run? They'll catch you, too. You couldn't, you know, do nothing about it. And I was just helpless sitting there. I couldn't do nothing about it. So later we used to send him packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: People used to meet him in the street in Brody. He used to go to work there in Brody. And I sent notes that he should run away. My mother sent notes. And he didn't want to run away, because he had a bad experience already. He said that he has food, and he's working there, and he's, he's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1943. And we don't know what happened to him. I mean, apparently, they, they killed him eventually there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that they liquidated in '43, when your mom ran away from the ghetto. What did they do? How was it liquidated?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took all the Jews to a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one? Do you happen to know where they sent them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Majdanek, I believe. I have to check that. In the book I wrote it down. Because they took-- they used to take, once in a while, some Jews. So at the beginning, they took him to another camp. But I-- they-- when they closed down the, the ghetto, they took them to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they burn the ghetto down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they burnt the hospital that your sister was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hospital they burned, yes. And, and people told us later, you know. I found out that afterwards-- after we survived, we got together with some people that were still in the ghetto and ran away later. So these people told us that the, the Germans were standing. And if some-- if the patients wanted to run out of the building, they used to shoot them. They wouldn't let them get, get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the point? They were very sick? Were they afraid of contagion? Why do you think they burnt it down? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think because they, they were sick. You know, you-- there was no reason why. They just did things, you know. They did whatever they felt like.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did you continue this hiding, running from place to place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hiding for a whole year. I came to the village in March 1943. And we were liberated in March 1944 by the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive in the winter, in the cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's amazing. I remember I had a shawl. I had a jacket, and the jacket was worn out. And the boots were wearing out. And this Keroltchikha gave me a piece of linen. She gave me flax and a big needle. And I sat in the attic. And I made myself like a vest. And I filled it. You know, I took piece of, two piece of cloth, like a lining in the top linen. And inside I put the flax. And that's how I made myself a vest that kept me warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just understand. They wouldn't let you stay in the house, in the houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were afraid to keep me in the house. If a neighbor came in then, you know-- to find out that she's keeping a Jew. Because if-- the order was from the Germans. If a non-Jew kept a Jew safe, a Jew, he would be killed. That was the order from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of any occasions that that happened in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I don't know of any occasions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know of any occasions that a family, a non-Jewish family in that town, denounced a Jew, told the authorities that a Jew was hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no. Not-- I know there was another family that was caught. And I was told that the neighbors-- oh, by the way, I have to go back to this. That first place that I went that my mother took out from jail, this woman, I was there-- I don't remember-- a few weeks, probably a few weeks. And then the woman told me that she couldn't keep me any longer. Because the neighbors are already talking that she's keeping a Jewish girl. I used to be in the room there in the house. She lived on the outskirts of the village. And she told me if somebody knocks on the door-- I used to hide, like behind the oven. We used to call it the [NON-ENGLISH]. That was like a, like a hiding place. So that's what I used to do. Now, after a few weeks, she told me to go. And I left. Later, I found out that another Jewish family came to her. He probably offered her money. They came, three people, a couple with a little girl. And so she told me to go. I used to be there in the house babysitting for her child. She had a baby. So she told me to go. Then I was told a story that the couple was there a few weeks, and the Germans came. And they ran away. They ran off. And while they running, the Germans shot them running away. So apparently, the neighbors told that she's keeping--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't shoot this woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they didn't shoot the woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: They threatened, but they didn't do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't shoot her. I mean that's-- I never seen that woman again. I did send them packages later on. They got in touch with me. But I never spoke to her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maslowska, Maslowska. I have a picture here of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said your house was on the main street of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: My house was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So hers was, too, if she was a neighbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was on the outskirts of town. So she was like two kilometers away from the center of the village. Yeah. And that's why she wasn't afraid to keep me in the house. Because she was away from other neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were staying there, you weren't paying her? She was doing this for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no money with me at all. All I had, my mother's marriage ring-- when we were packing to go to the ghetto, my mother gave me her marriage ring. And I sewed it in, into the jacket. And she said, in emergency, use that ring. In fact, there was an emergency, by the way. I was caught once. I used to go-- when it was warm, I used sit in the fields between traps. And many times I went to the woods. So I remember one early dawn. It was still dark a little. I walked toward the woods. And again, a guard from the woods came along. And he saw me. And he caught me. And he was taking me to the police, too. And while-- I walked, like, you know, a mile or so. And all of a sudden, some boys were running through the woods. And  he seen those boys running. So he left me. Because he had-- instead of one Jew, he had a few Jews. That was my luck. That's how I survived that time. He ran. And I remember running. It's like I'm still running there, [LAUGHS] you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how-- your feelings at this point. You were 16 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 16. And, I mean, the will to survive was very strong. I remember sitting in the woods one day. It was raining, like, all day. And I was soaked to the bone. And I couldn't run and get away from the woods. Because daytime I was afraid to go to the village, you know, to go into a barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sat for a whole day in that rain. And I felt, you know, I don't think I could take it anymore. Maybe I go back to the ghetto, but-- my family was still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I went back to the village. I dried my shirt and my jacket. I put it in the oven. You know, when the oven, wood-burning oven was burning, the, the wool was hot. And I would dry my clothes. And the next day I would go in the woods again. And sometimes it rained the next day again. But the will to survive was very strong, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you physically at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very healthy. I never had a cold. In fact, my mother was sickly, a sickly woman. She was never sick while she was hiding. So my mother was hiding, like, for nine months. She was hiding. And she never had a cold. Of course, after liberation, a few weeks after, she collapsed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you feel there was something--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was extra strength, unusual strength that we had. And many people-- I spoke to other people that they were in the woods, sleeping on, on the snow. And they didn't catch a cold. I-- you know, I remember many times I was freezing. It was cold. But in the hay, when you-- you know, I would go into the hay, cover myself with the hay. And that kept me warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your feeling about people? I mean, you were young, very impressionable person at that age. What did you think about all that was going on around you? Or did you have time to really think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a lot of time to think. I, I somehow don't remember my, my, my thoughts. It was-- it's like, you know, I mean, we had no other choice. And there was nothing-- you know, somehow, I don't know. I couldn't remember my thoughts then.

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, when I-- I show, give slide presentations, and I talk to children many times. And, and the children ask, what did you do all day when you were hiding? I was busy to, to-- nobody should come and find me. I was busy to survive, you know. That was the thought at the time. So you were constantly watching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just always watching. So even a, a bird flew by, you, you got nervous somebody's coming. Then I went to-- I heard of people being in the woods. And now when I think, how did I know how to find them? But you just walked, you know. I walked toward that direction in the woods. And I did find these people. And I was very happy. Because I was alone all that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met these people. And I sat down. And they were sitting-- on the floor there were leaves. And the leaves sort of kept them warmer from the dampness when it rained. They slept there. So these leaves were infested with lice. I mean, these people were there for months. And they didn't take a bath, and so forth. So they told me, if you were clean-- I mean, I had lice, too, but more or less under control. So they told me, move a little further. So you wouldn't get, catch, you know, the-- so I moved a little further. But I slept through the night there. And I was infested with lice. It was terrible. I never seen such, you know, big, big creatures.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed there for a day, and, and the next day I, I went home. I couldn't take it. I felt I'm better off alone than to be in the infested--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they accepted your presence there? They didn't tell you, find another place on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. These were people that I knew. They were neighbors from the next town, from the next town. They were neighbors that were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people help each other if they met each other? Did they just mind their own business? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, these people, you know-- I brought my own piece of bread with me. So I ate my own bread. And, and they didn't mind if I stayed there. But I remember another episode that I was hiding in an attic. And there was another man from our village. And at night, he came out to relieve himself. And I seen him. But I was afraid to go down. I was in the attic. He was downstairs, down in the snow. It was winter. And I was afraid to go down to talk to him. Because I felt if by whispering somebody could hear us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think people were very selfish at the time. Everybody was trying for himself most of the time. Mrs. Geller, the person that used to live in the ghetto with us, she was telling-- her daughter was telling me about an episode. And, and she was really-- it was like things her mother did that wasn't natural, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in a bunker. And Germans came around, and they started shooting them. And they ran away. So the mother left. She was four years-- the girl was four years at the time. So the mother ran out of the bunker, and she ran away. And the girl now, of course, 50 years. So she tells me she was shocked that her mother-- she was such a good mother and-- that she left her. But she did come back. She ran away, and then she realized that she left a little kid. So she came back. She grabbed her. And she was lucky. She ran away. She didn't get shot. But people did these things that they just thought of themselves. That's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very slim at this point, or did you have enough food to give you the strength to keep on going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had enough. There were days that I did starve. I used to make up-- the Orthodox people fast, like, Mondays-- was it Monday and Thursday or Tuesday or Thursday?

speaker: interviewer
text: Monday, Thursday

speaker: interviewee
text: Monday and Thursday. So I used to make a fast on Monday in order to save the piece of bread. So I didn't have to go down one day. I was safe sitting in the attic another-- for two days in a row. But most of the time I, I did have-- I didn't starve that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other times that you used the religious teachings in your life to help you get through? Like you said this sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to think. I don't remember of any that I was aware of any holidays. I don't think I was aware. We didn't know. We didn't have a calendar. I think we didn't know at times what day it was. I didn't follow the holidays, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever given ham to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In fact, when Keroltchikha brought me the soup, it was with ham.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ate it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I did. Because I was hungry, and I didn't think twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's move on just a bit. Now you're-- I guess we'll continue this on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
